concept_name: Force on a Moving Charge in Magnetic Field
locked_facts: Magnetic force on a moving charge: F = qv × B, magnitude F = qvB sin θ
Force is zero when v is parallel to B (θ = 0° or 180°)
Force is maximum when v is perpendicular to B (θ = 90°)
The magnetic force does NO work on the charged particle — it only changes direction, not speed
When v ⊥ B, the particle moves in a circle of radius r = mv/(qB)
Time period of circular motion: T = 2πm/(qB), independent of velocity
When v has components both parallel and perpendicular to B, the path is a HELIX
truth_statements: The magnetic force on a moving charge is always perpendicular to both the velocity and the magnetic field, as given by the cross product F = qv × B
Since the force is always perpendicular to the velocity, the magnetic force does zero work — kinetic energy and speed remain constant
A charged particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field traces a circular path with radius r = mv/(qB)
The time period T = 2πm/(qB) is independent of the particle's speed — faster particles trace larger circles but take the same time
If the velocity has a component along B, the particle follows a helical path with the pitch determined by the parallel velocity component

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two particles with different speeds enter the same magnetic field perpendicular to B. The faster particle traces a LARGER circle, but both complete one revolution in the SAME time T = 2πm/(qB). Speedometers on both particles show constant speed throughout.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Side-by-side circles of different radii — both particles reaching the 12 o'clock position simultaneously. The speed readout never changes, proving zero work by the magnetic force.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students expect the force to speed up or slow down the particle (like gravitational force on a projectile) — they see speed stays constant and only direction changes

simulation_states.label: Charge enters magnetic field
simulation_states.description: A positive charge q moving with velocity v enters a region of uniform magnetic field B (shown as × symbols for field into the page). The velocity vector and B field are clearly labelled.
simulation_states.emphasis: A charged particle with velocity v enters a region where B is perpendicular to v (into the page)

simulation_states.label: Force deflects — circular path begins
simulation_states.description: The force F = qv × B appears perpendicular to v (upward for positive charge moving right in B into page). The particle curves upward. The force vector rotates as v rotates, always pointing toward the centre of the emerging circle.
simulation_states.emphasis: The force is always perpendicular to v — it acts as a centripetal force, constantly changing direction but never changing speed

simulation_states.label: Complete circle — speed unchanged
simulation_states.description: The particle completes a full circle of radius r = mv/(qB). A speed readout shows constant |v| throughout. A second particle with double the speed enters and traces a circle of double the radius — but both have the same period T = 2πm/(qB).
simulation_states.emphasis: Speed never changes — magnetic force does ZERO work. Period is the same regardless of speed. Faster particle → bigger circle, same time.

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts charge q, velocity v, magnetic field B, and mass m. The circular path radius and period update in real time. A toggle allows switching to helical motion by adding a velocity component along B. Negative charge option shows reversed circular direction.
simulation_states.emphasis: Vary v: radius changes but period stays the same. Increase B: tighter circle, shorter period. Switch to negative charge: circle direction reverses.

common_misconceptions.belief: The magnetic force accelerates (speeds up) the charged particle
trigger_phrases: magnetic field se fast hota hai
common_misconceptions.correction: The magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does zero work (W = F·d = 0 when F ⊥ d). The speed and kinetic energy remain constant — only the direction changes. The force provides centripetal acceleration, not tangential acceleration.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show speed readout staying constant throughout the circular path — the velocity vector rotates but its magnitude never changes

common_misconceptions.belief: A stronger magnetic field makes the particle move faster in its circular path
common_misconceptions.correction: A stronger magnetic field decreases the radius (r = mv/qB) and the time period (T = 2πm/qB), making the circle tighter and the revolution faster. But the SPEED of the particle stays the same — it was determined by the initial velocity, not by B.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Increase B while keeping v constant — show the circle shrinking but the speed readout remaining unchanged

common_misconceptions.belief: The time period depends on the velocity of the particle
trigger_phrases: speed se time period change hota hai
common_misconceptions.correction: The time period T = 2πm/(qB) depends only on mass, charge, and field strength — NOT on velocity. A faster particle traces a proportionally larger circle, so it takes the same time for one revolution. This is the principle behind the cyclotron.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two particles with different speeds completing circles of different radii in the exact same time

legend.meaning: Field directed out of the page
